message.content:
In /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/reflow_report.py, implement **W5 — 4IR framing as an ORGANISING FRAME, not scenery**.

MEASURED (verify yourself; split body from '## References' first — a whole-file grep re-reads the bibliography
as prose and has produced three false findings already): the Rank10 BODY contains "Fourth Industrial Revolution"
x4 in 7,742 words, "4IR" x0, and ALL FOUR are name-drops. The task prompt MANDATES the framing: "Focus on how AI,
as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution...". It is graded THREE times — comprehensiveness "Grounding
in 4IR Context" (w=0.10), instruction-following "Integration of the 4IR theme" (w=0.15), insight "Insightful
Integration of 4IR" (w=0.15) — combined ~12.6% of the total score, currently earning near-floor.

The human reference OPENS with 4IR (subsection 1.1 defines it, positions AI as THE key driver, argues
technological interconnection, then REUSES that lens in later sections). Read /home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md
to see exactly how it does this.

Implement: reframe the introduction to position AI within the 4IR as the driver of labour restructuring, and
thread the frame through 3-4 section-initial topic sentences so it ORGANISES rather than decorates. These are
class-I/class-S sentences: "Fourth Industrial Revolution"/"4IR" is on the task-prompt CONCEPT_WHITELIST so naming
it is legal discourse — but any FACTUAL claim about 4IR still requires a verified FACT sentence. No digits, no
new entities, no [n] markers in injected sentences; they must pass validate_interpretation().
Paste the injected sentences as evidence.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

9	    ours  : 7,692 judge-read words | 12 paragraphs | avg 633 w/para | 0 H3 | 0 tables | 0 bullets
10	    ref   : 9,029 judge-read words | 59 paragraphs | avg 142 w/para | 24 H3 | 10 tables | 91 bullets
11	
12	and INSIGHT (0.32, the heaviest dimension, our worst) is structurally unreachable: the entailment
13	judge's NEUTRAL clause deletes any sentence that "introduces a fact, entity, MECHANISM ... NOT
14	present in the SPAN" -- which is the definition of an interpretive sentence.
15	
16	This stage rewrites the DOCUMENT without touching the writer or the verifier. It is a
17	report -> report transform over a banked artifact, so a full wave costs ZERO compose runs.
18	
19	THE FAITHFULNESS CONTRACT (deterministic, not model judgment)
20	------------------------------------------------------------
21	Every shipped sentence belongs to exactly one class:
22	
23	  CLASS F (FACT)           carries [n] citations and/or numbers. Must appear BYTE-IDENTICAL to a
24	                           sentence in the input. The validator asserts the multiset of fact
25	                           sentences is PRESERVED (regroup/reorder only). Never invented, never
26	                           mutated, never dropped.
27	  CLASS S (STRUCTURE)      headers, bullet markers, table cells. Table cells are assembled from
28	                           sidecar fields. `###` headings are the ONE piece of LLM-authored class-S
29	                           text, so every heading passes `validate_heading` (no digit, no citation,
30	                           no new proper noun, <= HEADING_MAX_WORDS); a heading that fails is DELETED
31	                           (never reverted-around, never shipped).
32	  CLASS I (INTERPRETATION) the ONLY new prose. Legal iff ALL of:
33	                             (1) contains NO digit, %, unit, or SPELLED-OUT quantity
34	                                 ("fourteen percent", "a third", "doubled", "tenfold")
35	                             (2) contains NO [n] citation marker and NO quotation mark
36	                             (3) introduces NO new proper noun (every capitalised token must already
37	                                 appear in the paragraph's FACT sentences or the concept whitelist)
38	                                 and NO un-named attribution ("according to", "analysts", "one study")
39	                             (4) uses an epistemic frame ("may", "suggests", "consistent with", ...)
40	                                 and NO universal/certainty/forecast claim ("all", "every", "will"),
41	                                 NO bare result assertion ("output rose", "employment fell") and NO
42	                                 negated finding ("no gain was observed") -- both of those are
43	                                 checkable claims wearing an interpretation's clothes
44	                             (5) sits adjacent to >= 2 FACT sentences citing >= 2 DISTINCT sources,
45	                                 points AT them deictically ("these findings", "taken together") and
46	                                 shares >= ANCHOR_MIN content stem with them
47	                             (6) <= 1 per paragraph, <= I_BUDGET_FRAC of body sentences
48	                             (7) survives a fail-closed CONTRADICTION screen against its premises
49	                                 (CONTRADICTED, judge exception, or the judge's
50	                                 ("ENTAILED", "judge_error: ...") SENTINEL => DROPPED, never kept)
51	
52	Any violation drops the offending sentence; any structural violation reverts the WHOLE PARAGRAPH /
53	SECTION / DOCUMENT to its source text. Fail-closed at every seam.
54	
55	The structural validator proves three things about the shipped body:
56	    a. the FACT-sentence multiset is identical to the source's (regroup/reorder only);
57	    b. every NON-fact sentence shipped is either byte-identical to a source sentence or is an
58	       APPROVED class-I sentence -- i.e. there is no other new prose anywhere;
59	    c. the prose word count did not grow beyond source + the approved class-I sentences.
60	
61	GUARANTEE: the worst content that can ship is a hedged, uncited, capped, contradiction-screened
62	relational gloss over already-verified facts. A fabricated number, quote or attribution cannot pass
63	the deterministic string checks -- it is not a matter of the model being careful.
64	
65	ONE HONEST CONCESSION (see ANCHOR_MIN): the premise-overlap floor is 1 content stem, not 2. At 2 it
66	is UNSATISFIABLE for the sentence class this whole stage exists to produce -- a reconciliation names
67	the MECHANISM behind two findings, and a mechanism is by construction vocabulary the findings do not
68	contain. That is the same failure the entailment judge's NEUTRAL clause has. Relevance is instead
69	enforced by the deictic-pointer requirement (5) plus the fail-closed contradiction screen (7); every
70	other rule here is strictly stronger than the version it replaced.

199	def split_sentences(text: str) -> list[str]:
200	    """Whitespace-normalised sentence split.

200 """Whitespace-normalised sentence split. 201 202 Normalising runs of whitespace to a single space FIRST is what makes the structural validator 203 invariant to REGROUPING: the same fact sentence must compare equal whether the source had it 204 inside a 600-word paragraph (wrapped over newlines) or the reflow put it on its own bullet. 205 """ 206 t = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip() 207 if not t: 208 return [] 209 parts = SENT_BOUNDARY_RE.split(t) 210 sents: list[str] = [] 211 # parts == [chunk, terminator, chunk, terminator, ..., tail] 212 for i in range(0, len(parts) - 1, 2): 213 s = (parts[i] + parts[i + 1]).strip() 214 if s: 215 sents.append(s) 216 tail = parts[-1].strip() 217 if tail: 218 sents.append(tail) 219 return sents 220 221 222 def split_body_refs(md: str) -> tuple[str, str]: 223 """The report ENDS with a numbered '## References' list. A regex over the whole file 224 re-reads the bibliography as prose -- that trap has produced three false findings already.""" 225 m = re.search(r"\n#+\s*References\s*\n", md, re.I) 226 if not m: 227 return md, "" 228 return md[: m.start()], md[m.start():] 229 230 231 def is_fact_sentence(s: str) -> bool: 232 """FACT = carries a citation marker or a number. Only these may assert anything checkable.""" 233 return bool(CITE_RE.search(s)) or bool(DIGIT_RE.search(s)) 234 235 236 def cited_ids(s: str) -> set[str]: 237 return set(CITE_RE.findall(s)) 238 239 240 def strip_md(text: str) -> str: 241 """Remove markdown chrome so sentence comparison is on prose only.""" 242 t = re.sub(r"^#{1,6}\s+.*$", "", text, flags=re.M) # headers (class S) 243 t = re.sub(r"^\s*\|.*$", "", t, flags=re.M) # tables (class S) 244 t = re.sub(r"^\s*[-*+]\s+", "", t, flags=re.M) # bullet markers (content kept) 245 t = re.sub(r"^\s*\d+\.\s+", "", t, flags=re.M) # ordered-list markers 246 t = re.sub(r"\*\*|__|\*", "", t) # bold/italic 247 return t 248 249 250 def _stem(w: str) -> str: 251 for suf in ("ations", "ation", "ments", "ment", "ings", "ing", "ions", "ion", "ives", "ive", 252 "ers", "ies", "ed", "es", "s"): 253 if w.endswith(suf) and len(w) - len(suf) >= 4: 254 return w[: -len(suf)] 255 return w 256 257 258 def content_stems(text: str) -> set[str]: 259 """Content-bearing stems: >=4 alphabetic chars, stopwords removed.""" 260 return { 261 _stem(w) for w in re.findall(r"[a-z]{4,}", text.lower()) if w not in STOPWORDS 262 } 263 264 265 def _hits(patterns, low: str) -> str | None: 266 for p in patterns: 267 m = re.search(p, low, re.I) 268 if m: 269 return m.group(0).strip() 270 return None 271 272 273 # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- class-I validator 274 275 def validate_interpretation(sent: str, premises: list[str]) -> tuple[bool, str]: 276 """Deterministic gate for a CLASS I sentence. No LLM. 277 278 Returns (ok, reasons). ALL violated rules are reported, not just the first -- an attack that 279 trips three rules should say so, otherwise a rule can silently rot behind an earlier one (the 280 old code reported 'no_epistemic_frame' for the Goldman-Sachs attack and the proper-noun rule 281 was never actually exercised by the suite). 282 """ 283 s = sent.strip() 284 if not s: 285 return False, "empty" 286 low = s.lower() 287 bad: list[str] = [] 288 289 # (1) no checkable quantity -- digits, units, or the same number spelled out in words 290 if DIGIT_RE.search(s): 291 bad.append("contains_digit") 292 if UNIT_RE.search(s): 293 bad.append("contains_unit") 294 h = _hits(NUMBER_WORDS_BANNED, low) 295 if h: 296 bad.append(f"spelled_out_quantity:{h}") 297 298 # (2) no citation marker (never masquerade as verified), no quotation (attributable speech) 299 if CITE_RE.search(s): 300 bad.append("carries_citation_marker") 301 if QUOTE_RE.search(s): 302 bad.append("contains_quotation") 303 304 # (4) epistemic frame required; certainty, forecasts, result assertions and negated findings banned 305 if not any(re.search(rf"\b{re.escape(e)}\b", low) for e in EPISTEMIC): 306 bad.append("no_epistemic_frame") 307 h = _hits(CERTAINTY_BANNED, low) 308 if h: 309 bad.append(f"certainty_or_overclaim:{h}") 310 h = _hits(RESULT_VERBS_BANNED, low) 311 if h: 312 bad.append(f"bare_result_assertion:{h}") 313 h = _hits(NEGATED_FINDING_BANNED, low) 314 if h: 315 bad.append(f"negated_finding:{h}") 316 317 # (3) attribution smuggled in without a proper noun 318 h = _hits(ATTRIBUTION_BANNED, low) 319 if h: 320 bad.append(f"unnamed_attribution:{h}") 321 322 # (5) must interpret >= 2 facts from >= 2 distinct sources 323 facts = [p for p in premises if is_fact_sentence(p)] 324 srcs: set[str] = set() 325 for p in facts: 326 srcs |= cited_ids(p) 327 if len(facts) < 2 or len(srcs) < 2: 328 bad.append(f"insufficient_premises(facts={len(facts)},sources={len(srcs)})") 329 330 premise_blob = " ".join(premises) 331 332 # (3) no new proper noun: every capitalised token must already appear in the premises 333 premise_caps = set(CAP_TOKEN_RE.findall(premise_blob)) 334 for t in CAP_TOKEN_RE.findall(s): 335 if t in CONCEPT_WHITELIST or t in premise_caps: 336 continue 337 # a sentence-initial capital of a word that is otherwise lowercase in the premises is fine 338 if s.startswith(t) and re.search(rf"\b{re.escape(t.lower())}\b", premise_blob.lower()): 339 continue 340 bad.append(f"new_proper_noun:{t}") 341 break 342 343 # (5) it must POINT AT these premises and share vocabulary with them 344 if not any(d in low for d in DEICTIC): 345 bad.append("no_deictic_pointer") 346 if len(content_stems(low) & content_stems(premise_blob)) < ANCHOR_MIN: 347 bad.append("insufficient_premise_anchor") 348 349 if bad: 350 return False, ",".join(bad) 351 return True, "" 352 353 354 # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- class-S heading gate 355 356 def validate_heading(heading_text: str, premises: list[str]) -> tuple[bool, str]: 357 """`###` headings are the only LLM-authored class-S text -- gate them like class-I lite. 358 359 A heading cannot hedge, so no epistemic frame is required; but it may not carry a number, a 360 citation, or a name that is not already in the section's facts. 361 """ 362 h = heading_text.strip().lstrip("#").strip() 363 if not h: 364 return False, "empty" 365 bad: list[str] = [] 366 if DIGIT_RE.search(h): 367 bad.append("contains_digit") 368 if UNIT_RE.search(h): 369 bad.append("contains_unit") 370 if CITE_RE.search(h): 371 bad.append("carries_citation_marker") 372 if len(h.split()) > HEADING_MAX_WORDS: 373 bad.append(f"too_long({len(h.split())}w)") 374 hit = _hits(CERTAINTY_BANNED, h.lower()) 375 if hit: 376 bad.append(f"certainty_or_overclaim:{hit}") 377 blob = " ".join(premises) 378 caps = set(CAP_TOKEN_RE.findall(blob)) 379 for t in CAP_TOKEN_RE.findall(h): 380 if t in CONCEPT_WHITELIST or t in caps: 381 continue 382 if h.startswith(t) and re.search(rf"\b{re.escape(t.lower())}\b", blob.lower()): 383 continue 384 bad.append(f"new_proper_noun:{t}") 385 break 386 return (not bad), ",".join(bad) 387 388 389 def gate_headings(section_md: str, premises: list[str]) -> tuple[str, int]: 390 """DELETE any `###` heading that fails the gate. Deleting a class-S line cannot touch prose.""" 391 out: list[str] = [] 392 dropped = 0 393 for line in section_md.split("\n"): 394 if re.match(r"^\s*###+\s+\S", line): 395 ok, why = validate_heading(line, premises) 396 if not ok: 397 print(f" [heading REJECTED] {why}: {line.strip()[:70]!r}") 398 dropped += 1 399 continue 400 out.append(line) 401 return "\n".join(out), dropped 402 403 404 # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- structural validator 405 406 def validate_reflow( 407 src_body: str, out_body: str, approved_i: list[str] | None = None 408 ) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]: 409 """The load-bearing check. Any failure => caller reverts to source. 410 411 (a) the FACT-sentence multiset is IDENTICAL (regroup/reorder only -- never invented, mutated, 412 dropped); 413 (b) every NON-fact sentence shipped is byte-identical to a source sentence or is an APPROVED 414 class-I sentence -- so no other new prose can exist anywhere in the document; 415 (c) prose did not grow beyond source + approved class-I words. Word counts are taken on 416 strip_md'd prose: markdown chrome (headings, bullet markers, table pipes) is class S and is 417 deliberately outside the budget, otherwise "regroup into bullets" is impossible by 418 construction. 419 """ 420 errs: list[str] = [] 421 approved = [re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s).strip() for s in (approved_i or [])] 422 423 src_prose = strip_md(src_body) 424 out_prose = strip_md(out_body) 425 src_sents = split_sentences(src_prose) 426 out_sents = split_sentences(out_prose) 427 428 # TRUE multiset diff (Counter, not `in`): a DUPLICATED fact sentence is present in both lists, 429 # so a membership test reports neither a loss nor an addition and the duplication slips through 430 # on to the word-count check alone. Counter subtraction catches all three: loss, invention, dup. 431 src_facts = Counter(s for s in src_sents if is_fact_sentence(s)) 432 out_facts = Counter(s for s in out_sents if is_fact_sentence(s)) 433 if src_facts != out_facts: 434 missing = list((src_facts - out_facts).elements()) 435 added = list((out_facts - src_facts).elements()) 436 if missing: 437 errs.append(f"FACT_SENTENCE_LOST({len(missing)}): {missing[0][:90]!r}") 438 if added: 439 dup = [s for s in added if s in src_facts] 440 tag = "FACT_SENTENCE_DUPLICATED" if dup else "FACT_SENTENCE_INVENTED_OR_MUTATED" 441 errs.append(f"{tag}({len(added)}): {added[0][:90]!r}") 442 443 # (b) no unapproved new prose 444 src_nonfact = {s for s in src_sents if not is_fact_sentence(s)} 445 for s in out_sents: 446 if is_fact_sentence(s) or s in src_nonfact: 447 continue 448 if any(s == a or a in s for a in approved): 449 continue 450 errs.append(f"UNAPPROVED_NEW_PROSE: {s[:90]!r}") 451 break 452 453 # (c) prose budget 454 budget = len(src_prose.split()) + sum(len(a.split()) for a in approved) 455 if len(out_prose.split()) > budget: 456 errs.append( 457 f"BODY_GREW: {len(src_prose.split())}(+{budget - len(src_prose.split())} approved) " 458 f"-> {len(out_prose.split())} words" 459 ) 460 return (not errs), errs 461 462 463 # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- W4: confession purge 464 465 CONFESSIONS = [ 466 r"[^.]*No contradictions were detected by the pipeline[^.]*\.", 467 r"[^.]*\bthe pipeline\b[^.]*\.", 468 r"[^.]*\btelemetry\b[^.]*\.", 469 r"[^.]*\bspan-grounded\b[^.]*\.", 470 r"[^.]*is detailed under [^.]*\.", 471 r"\(also mirrored\)", 472 r"\(tier [A-Z0-9]+\)", 473 ] 474 475 476 def purge_confessions(body: str) -> tuple[str, int]: 477 """Delete pipeline-confession sentences -- but NEVER touch a FACT sentence. 478 479 The purge runs BEFORE the structural baseline is taken, so anything it deletes is invisible to 480 validate_reflow. The old version ran `[^.]*\\bthe pipeline\\b[^.]*\\.` over the raw body: that 481 pattern crosses newlines and would silently delete a CITED sentence that happened to mention a 482 pipeline, with no validator left to catch it. rule (6): global cap -- anything over budget is dropped, deterministically, in order 968 room = max(0, budget - len(all_kept)) 969 if len(screened) > room: 970 for _, s, _ in screened[room:]: 971 print(f" [class-I REJECTED] over_global_budget: {s[:70]!r}") 972 screened = screened[:room] 973 974 cleaned = assemble(cleaned_paras, screened) 975 approved = [s for _, s, _ in screened] 976 977 ok, errs = validate_reflow(content, cleaned, approved_i=approved) 978 if not ok: 979 print(f" [{title[:40]}] VALIDATOR FAILED -> REVERT: {errs[0][:110]}") 980 out_sections.append(f"{title}\n{content}") 981 continue 982 h3 = len(re.findall(r"(?m)^###\s", cleaned)) 983 bl = len(re.findall(r"(?m)^\s*[-*]\s+\S", cleaned)) 984 print(f" [{title[:40]}] OK H3={h3} (dropped {nheads}) bullets={bl} class-I kept={len(approved)}") 985 all_kept.extend(approved) 986 out_sections.append(cleaned if cleaned.lstrip().startswith("#") else f"{title}\n{cleaned}") 987 988 new_body = (header.rstrip() + "\n\n" if header.strip() else "") + "\n\n".join(out_sections) 989 990 # W3 — deterministic study table (class S) 991 if a.bib: 992 bib = json.loads(Path(a.bib).read_text()) 993 table = build_study_table(bib, new_body) 994 if table: 995 m = re.search(r"(?m)^##\s+.*$", new_body) 996 if m: 997 idx = new_body.find("\n", m.end()) 998 new_body = new_body[:idx] + "\n" + table + new_body[idx:] 999 print(f"[W3] study table inserted ({table.count(chr(10) + '|') - 1} rows)") 1000 1001 # FINAL global validator — the whole body, fail-closed 1002 ok, errs = validate_reflow(body, new_body, approved_i=all_kept) 1003 if not ok: 1004 print(f"[FATAL] global validator failed -> writing SOURCE unchanged: {errs}") 1005 new_body = body 1006 all_kept = [] 1007 1008 # The audit is the ONLY record a human reads before trusting the score, so it may never 1009 # overstate the document: reconcile it to what is actually on disk. 1010 shipped = [s for s in all_kept if re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s).strip() in re.sub(r"\s+", " ", new_body)] 1011 if len(shipped) != len(all_kept): 1012 print(f"[BUG] {len(all_kept) - len(shipped)} class-I sentence(s) were counted as kept but are " 1013 f"NOT in the shipped body -- audit reconciled to the document") 1014 all_kept = shipped 1015 1016 Path(a.out).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) 1017 Path(a.out).write_text(new_body.rstrip() + "\n\n" + refs.lstrip("\n")) 1018 1019 audit = { 1020 "source": a.inp, 1021 "src_body_words": src_words, 1022 "out_body_words": len(new_body.split()), 1023 "confessions_purged": npurged, 1024 "class_I_budget": budget, 1025 "h3_subsections": len(re.findall(r"(?m)^###\s", new_body)), 1026 "bullets": len(re.findall(r"(?m)^\s*[-*]\s+\S", new_body)), 1027 "tables": len(re.findall(r"(?m)^\|\s*[-:]{3,}", new_body)), 1028 "class_I_kept": all_kept, 1029 "class_I_count": len(all_kept), 1030 } 1031 if a.audit: 1032 Path(a.audit).write_text(json.dumps(audit, indent=1)) 1033 print(json.dumps({k: v for k, v in audit.items() if k != "class_I_kept"}, indent=1)) 1034 print(f"[audit] {len(all_kept)} class-I sentences kept -- EVERY ONE must be read before the score is trusted") 1035 return 0 1036 1037 1038 if __name__ == "__main__": 1039 raise SystemExit(main())

5:## Introduction and Scope of the Evidence

25:## Distributional Impacts: Inequality, Job Quality, and the Digital Divide

=== HEADER/first 40 lines ===
# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market

This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.

## Introduction and Scope of the Evidence

Within this framework, automation generates a displacement effect that reduces labor's share of value added, countered by a reinstatement effect as new tasks reinstate labor into a broader range of activities, and the net impact depends on the relative magnitudes of these opposing forces.[1] The contemporary context for these dynamics is the Fourth Industrial Revolution, characterized by a fusion of technologies blurring the lines between the physical, digital, and biological spheres and evolving at an exponential rather than linear pace.[2] Within this revolution, AI and smart robotics are described as emerging technologies that dramatically change not only productivity and the ways of work but also the types and number of available jobs.[3] The Fourth Industrial Revolution is also framed as a paradigm of cyber-physical systems constituted by general-purpose technologies applied across multiple industries, including artificial intelligence, blockchain, genomics, and the Internet of Things.[4] Empirically, a study tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 found that when AI can perform most tasks comprising a particular job, the share of people in that role within a company falls by about 14 percent, but when AI's impact is concentrated in just a few tasks, employment in that role can grow.[5] Even workers in high-wage roles heavily exposed to AI saw their share of total employment grow by about 3 percent over five years, because AI boosted firm productivity and companies that used the technology grew faster.[5] A large increase in AI use is linked to about 6 percent higher employment growth and 9.5 percent more sales growth over five years, suggesting that firms adopting AI do not necessarily shed workers but can grow and use workers more efficiently.[5] Exposure to AI is greatest in high-paying roles, contrasting with earlier technology waves from the 1980s through early 2000s that mainly displaced middle-skill, routine jobs.[5] Business, financial, architecture, and engineering jobs shrank by about 2 percent to 2.5 percent over five years because of their high share of tasks matching what AI can do.[5] At a broader structural level, research measuring changes in the US labor market going back over a century found that the pace of occupational change has slowed over time, with the period from 1990 to 2017 being less disruptive than any prior period measured back to 1880.[6] The total number of jobs in the US economy grew by 14.5 percent between 2013 and 2023, while the number of light delivery service truck drivers grew by 29 percent and the occupation of stockers and order fillers grew from 1.8 million to more than 2.8 million jobs, an increase of nearly 60 percent.[6] These estimates further project that 11.8 million workers currently employed in declining demand occupations will be forced to change jobs by 2030.[7] The World Economic Forum has predicted that the adoption of AI will make 75 million jobs redundant and create 133 million new ones worldwide by 2022.[8] International Data Corporation projected that global spending in AI would reach $85.3 billion in 2021 and grow to more than $204 billion in 2025, representing a compound annual growth rate of 24.5 percent for the period 2021 through 2025.[8] Economic analyses by PwC, IBM, Deloitte, and Gartner Research suggest that adoption of AI will increase global GDP by 15 percent by 2030, representing an additional $15.7 trillion.[8] Despite these projections, a productivity paradox persists: aggregate labor productivity growth in the US averaged only 1.3 percent per year from 2005 to 2016, less than half the 2.8 percent annual growth rate sustained from 1995 to 2004.[9] AI's technological capabilities have advanced markedly, with error rates in labeling the content of photos on ImageNet falling from over 30 percent in 2010 to less than 5 percent in 2016 and as low as 2.2 percent, while error rates in voice recognition on the Switchboard corpus improved from 8.5 percent to 5.5 percent.[9] The scope of disruption extends across industries, with technological disruptions such as the Internet of Things, autonomous devices, enhanced analytical capabilities through AI, and rich media creating smart environments that are transforming industry structures, processes, and practices in tourism and hospitality.[10] In the MENA region specifically, new technologies including automation, robotics, and artificial intelligence are described as disruptive forces causing structural unemployment and inequalities among and within nations.[11] The Fourth Industrial Revolution is projected to alter the share of new occupations from 16 percent to 27 percent by 2022 while redundant jobs decline from 31 percent to 21 percent, with an estimated 0.98 million jobs lost and 1.74 million created.[12] This revolution builds on the Third Industrial Revolution's digital foundations and is distinguished by its scale, scope, and complexity, demanding integrated and comprehensive responses from all stakeholders including the public and private sectors.[2] Research indicates that the number of jobs for clerks in the USA may decrease by 1.6 million and retail salespeople by 830,000.[7]

## Theoretical Foundations: Task-Based Models of Automation and Reinstatement

This framework, building on prior contributions including Autor, Levy, and Murnane (2003), Acemoglu and Autor (2011), and Zeira (1998), posits that production requires tasks allocated to either capital or labor, and that technological change alters the task content of production rather than simply augmenting factor productivity.[1][13] (also mirrored) Within this framework, automation corresponds to the development of technologies enabling capital to substitute for labor in specific tasks, generating what the authors term a "displacement effect" that reduces labor's share of value added.[1] (also mirrored) Simultaneously, automation generates a "productivity effect" by increasing value added and raising demand for labor in non-automated tasks, meaning the net impact on labor demand depends on the relative magnitudes of these two forces.[1] (also mirrored) The framework identifies a third mechanism—the "reinstatement effect"—whereby new technologies create labor-intensive tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage, directly increasing the labor share and functioning as the polar opposite of the displacement effect.[1][13] (also mirrored) During this period, wage bill per capita grew at 2.5 percent per year, largely explained by the productivity effect of 2.4 percent per year.[16][13] (also mirrored) However, from 1987 to 2017, the pattern shifted markedly: the displacement effect accelerated to 0.7 percent per year while the reinstatement effect decelerated to 0.35 percent per year, generating a significant negative shift in the task content of production against labor.[16][13] (also mirrored) Cumulatively, changes in the task content of production reduced labor demand by 10 percent during this later period, and wage bill growth slowed to 1.33 percent per year, essentially stagnating since 2000.[13][14] In manufacturing, the displacement effect was particularly pronounced, reducing labor demand at about 1.1 percent per year or approximately 30 percent cumulatively.[16][13] (also mirrored) Autor further notes that in 1900, 41 percent of the US workforce was employed in agriculture, falling to 2 percent by 2000, illustrating the historical scale of automation-driven displacement.[17] Establishments whose task structures in 2010 were more suitable for AI subsequently posted relatively more AI vacancies, with a standardized coefficient of 9.19 and standard error of 1.21, suggesting that AI adoption follows task-based exposure patterns.[18] Short-term evidence from an online labor market provides striking results: following the release of ChatGPT, freelancers in highly affected occupations experienced a 2 percent decrease in monthly jobs and a 5.2 percent decrease in monthly earnings.[19] Image-based generative AI models produced similar effects, with a 2.1 percent reduction in monthly jobs and a 5.2 percent reduction in monthly earnings, suggesting that the displacement effect operates consistently across different AI modalities.[19] That study identifies "techies"—engineers and technicians with strong STEM skills—as a key factor: economies abundant in techies experienced strong skill upgrading, while polarization persisted in economies with few techies, and robotization was associated with skill upgrading in manufacturing.[20] In the Chinese context, digital transformation research finds that digitalization is associated with increased hiring in managerial, professional, and technical roles and reduced demand for auxiliary and manual labor, with abstract task demand rising while routine and manual tasks decline.[21] One analysis observes that digitalization represents a general-purpose technology whose net effect on employment will likely be negative, though the extent to which this is offset by the creation of new types of goods and services remains uncertain.[22] Another study employs a nuanced task-based approach to show that AI is reshaping inequality through complex interactions of skill restructuring, wage decoupling from productivity, and shifts in worker bargaining power, potentially accelerating capital-biased technological change.[23] Acemoglu and Restrepo developed a task-based framework for understanding automation's impact on labor markets, published in the Journal of Economic Perspectives.[1] (also mirrored) The distinction among these effects is essential because, as the authors emphasize, not all automation technologies increase aggregate labor demand; some automation technologies may reduce labor demand because they bring sizable displacement effects but only modest productivity gains.[1] Between 1947 and 1987, the displacement effect reduced labor demand by approximately 0.48 percent per year, while the reinstatement effect increased it by 0.47 percent per year, resulting in small changes in the task content of production.[16][13] (also mirrored) Acemoglu and Restrepo estimate that about half of employment growth over 1980–2015 took place in occupations in which job titles or tasks performed by workers changed.[1] Complementing this theoretical framework, Autor demonstrates that tasks which cannot be substituted by automation are generally complemented by it.[17] Autor's analysis of automated teller machines illustrates this complementarity: ATM numbers quadrupled from approximately 100,000 to 400,000 between 1995 and 2010, yet bank teller employment rose modestly from 500,000 to approximately 550,000 over a 30-year period.[17] As the routine cash-handling tasks of bank tellers receded, information technology enabled bank personnel to become involved in "relationship banking".[17] Legal occupations, which face little direct impact from automation and are often in firms that use AI heavily, saw a predicted 6.4% increase in employment.[5] The cited work presents a task-based framework for analyzing technology's implications for labor demand and productivity, including how technology displaces and reinstates labor.[1][13] (also mirrored)

## Occupational Exposure to AI: Measures and Susceptibility

Early foundational work by Frey and Osborne estimated the probability of computerisation for 702 detailed occupations using a Gaussian process classifier, establishing a task-based exposure methodology that became dominant in the field of AI labor market research.[24][25] Building on this paradigm, Felten, Raj, and Seamans developed a novel dataset measuring occupational, industry, and geographic exposure to artificial intelligence, published in the Strategic Management Journal.[26] Their approach, unlike the original Frey and Osborne framework, allowed temporal updating but still did not distinguish between AI types such as generative AI versus symbolic AI.[25] That study estimated that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces and general training.[27] When accounting for current and likely future software developments that complement LLM capabilities, this share jumps to just over 46% of jobs.[27] A separate text-mining analysis of the International Standard Occupation Classification found that 32.8% of occupations could be fully impacted by ChatGPT, while 36.5% might experience a partial impact and 30.7% are likely to remain unaffected.[28] For the 32.8% of occupations facing a full impact, the effects may be particularly pronounced and disruptive, whereas the 30.7% with no impact will likely continue performing their jobs as before.[28] That study found that the cognitive abilities most exposed to the recent surge of AI research are ideas-related, including attention and search, comprehension and expression, and logical reasoning.[29] This generative AI era finding calls into question pre-GenAI assessments of which workers and tasks are most exposed, as earlier automation primarily targeted routine tasks while GenAI can execute complex, nonstandardized functions traditionally reliant on human judgment.[29][30] The O*NET framework used to classify occupational tasks defines core tasks as those with a relevance rating greater than 67 percent and a mean importance rating of more than 3.0.[30] Global business investment in AI increased 13-fold from US$14.57 billion in 2013 to US$189 billion in 2023, with 35% of businesses already using AI in their operations and more than 50% intending to implement AI technologies.[31] However, more recent data show that the share of STEM jobs grew from 6.5 percent in 2010 to nearly 10 percent in 2024, representing an almost 50 percent increase.[32] Between 2013 and 2023, the share of retail sales jobs dropped from 7.5 to 5.7 percent of the job market, a reduction of 25 percent, with the e-commerce sector identified as an early adopter of predictive AI.[32] By 2023, the International Federation of Robotics reported that average robot density in manufacturing worldwide reached a new high of 141 robots per 10,000 employees.[33] The same study found that job replacement risk is highest in manufacturing and retail, closely followed by the transportation and materials sector, while healthcare and education will proportionately gain more than they lose from automation.[33] Jobs for AI and Machine Learning specialists rose at a compounding rate of 32% per year between 2016 and 2023, illustrating the dual nature of AI as both a destroyer and creator of employment.[33] A critical survey of the empirical literature on generative AI's labor market impact found that no consistent evidence has yet emerged of systemic labor market disruption attributable to generative AI.[25] One study using a difference-in-differences design over 1.2 million contracts on Upwork found that in the five months after ChatGPT's release, treated occupations experienced up to a 20% reduction in earnings.[25] Recent meta-analytic studies confirmed the absence of statistically significant average effects of AI adoption on employment or wages and detected no robust signs of publication bias.[25] Displacement occurs in certain occupations, especially within digital freelance markets, while other sectors exhibit productivity gains or wage premia, reflecting the still-emergent diffusion of AI and its uneven adoption across industries and countries.[25] A risk management perspective emphasizes that the extensive acceptance of AI is likely to result in employment displacement, as many previously human-performed tasks become automatable, necessitating investment in education to facilitate workforce transitions into new roles.[34] In the pharmaceutical sector, the adoption of generative AI could lead to an annual increase in value added by 2.6 to 4.5 percent, primarily via assistance with research and development of new drugs.[35] By 2018, the company had expanded to over 1,500 Frontend Assistants supporting 4 million monthly transactions, performing tasks such as checking connections, entering orders, generating invoices, and processing cancellations.[36] A global AI conceptual framework developed within the International Futures platform produces dynamic forecasts for 186 countries, modeling interconnected aspects of human development including economics, demographics, and energy, providing a macro-level system for understanding long-range AI impacts.[37] Because the associated information processing and problem-solving tasks are the most transversal across occupations, the analysis documented an exponential increase in AI exposure across all occupational categories, even though comparatively high-skilled occupations are more exposed than elementary occupations.[29] A systematic literature review covering publications from 2015 to July 2025 identified research on AI and job displacement with major contributions from China, the US, and Germany.[31] An organizational study of automation found divergent perspectives between management and employees: business leaders rated workflow simplification positively with a mean of 4.19, while employees rated it 4.03, and work-life balance was rated much lower by employees at 2.26 compared to 2.99 by business leaders.[36] ALMATO deployed around 400 software bots by 2016, automating more than 40 processes across 30 different applications and handling roughly 320,000 business transactions each month.[36] Researchers have employed varied methodologies, including Gaussian process classifiers, text-mining approaches, benchmark-linked cognitive ability mapping, and integrated assessment platforms, to assess occupational exposure to computerisation and AI technologies.[24][28][29][37]

## Generative AI: Early Empirical Evidence on Employment and Productivity

The study also documents a 1.2 percentage point decrease in the probability of receiving any employment in a given month—approximately a 10% drop relative to baseline—and a 4.7% reduction in the number of jobs conditional on being employed.[38] By contrast, aggregate labor-market indicators through 2024–2025 show limited economywide disruption; one analysis reports that 35.9% of U.S. workers used generative AI by December 2025 but finds only small positive wage effects and no statistically significant declines in job openings or employment in exposed occupations.[39] A prominent Harvard University study tracking 62 million workers across 285,000 U.S. firms finds that junior positions have been shrinking at companies integrating AI since 2023, with researchers warning that AI is eroding the bottom rungs of career ladders.[40] This finding is consistent with online labor market evidence that entry-level and less-experienced workers in highly exposed occupations bear the brunt of early displacement effects.[38][39] In quantitative terms, treated participants completed the post-treatment task 11 minutes faster—a 0.75 standard deviation reduction—while evaluator grades increased by 0.45 standard deviations.[41] The experiment also documents a reduction in productivity inequality: the correlation between first-task and second-task grades fell from 0.41 in the control group to 0.14 in the treatment group, driven by larger gains for participants who scored lower initially.[41] Workers exposed to ChatGPT were 2x as likely to report using it in their real job two weeks after the experiment and 1.6x as likely two months after.[41] In a large-scale field study, the staggered introduction of a generative AI conversational assistant to 5,172 customer-support agents increased productivity, measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average.[42] Field experiments in online retail similarly document treatment effects ranging from 0% to 16.3% in sales, with smaller and newer sellers as well as less experienced consumers exhibiting disproportionately larger gains.[43] A comprehensive review of the empirical literature finds task-completion time reductions of 15% to more than 50% across writing, customer support, software development, accounting, law, and translation.[39] In software development specifically, GitHub Copilot users complete coding tasks 55.8% faster in controlled settings, with larger gains among less experienced developers.[39] A separate study of nearly 5,000 developers across three field experiments finds a 26.08% increase in weekly task completion, again driven by higher adoption and disproportionately larger gains for junior developers.[39] In a randomized trial with 300 professional translators, a tenfold increase in training compute reduced completion time by 12.3%, improved quality by 0.18 standard deviations, and raised earnings per minute by 16.1%, with lower-skilled translators experiencing gains roughly four times larger than higher-skilled peers.[39] These productivity gains exhibit a consistent pattern of skill compression, whereby less-experienced and lower-skilled workers benefit disproportionately from AI assistance.[39][41][42] Exposure measures across multiple studies converge toward high-wage jobs, with correlations between AI exposure and wages ranging from 0.27 to 0.54.[44] A separate review finds that automating firms show 2% to 10% higher employment and positive elasticities between 0.2% and 2%, suggesting complementarity at the firm level even where aggregate displacement is detectable.[45] The analysis finds that a 1% increase in freelancers' earnings is associated with an additional 0.5% reduction in the number of jobs and a 1.7% reduction in monthly income for text-based services.[38]

3:## **1\. Introduction: AI, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and Labor Market Restructuring**
5:### **1.1 Defining the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) and AI's Role**
7:The contemporary economic landscape is undergoing a profound transformation often referred to as the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) or Industry 4.0. This revolution signifies a fundamental shift driven by the convergence and proliferation of interconnected digital technologies, including artificial intelligence (AI), robotics, the Internet of Things (IoT), cloud computing, big data analytics, and cyber-physical systems. Unlike previous industrial revolutions characterized by mechanization, mass production, or early automation, the 4IR represents a new paradigm in industrial, economic, and social systems, marked by the fusion of physical, digital, and biological spheres. Its defining characteristics are the unprecedented *speed and scale* of technological change, raising significant concerns about the potential for radical transformations in workplaces, business models, and societal structures. Some analyses suggest AI, in particular, possesses a potential for unusually rapid diffusion compared to historical general-purpose technologies, amplifying these concerns.
9:Within this transformative context, Artificial Intelligence is consistently identified not merely as one technology among many, but as a core component and key driver. AI, broadly encompassing capabilities like machine learning (ML), natural language processing (NLP), computer vision, robotics, and complex problem-solving aimed at mimicking or exceeding human cognitive functions for specific tasks, serves as a central engine of the 4IR. Its role is particularly emphasized in enabling data-driven decision-making, optimizing complex systems and processes, fostering industrial innovation, and ultimately contributing to potential social advances. Recent advancements have brought prediction capabilities to the forefront, recasting AI's economic role as a significant drop in the cost of prediction, alongside the emergence of powerful generative AI models capable of creating novel content.

### **1.2 AI as a Catalyst for Labor Market Transformation**

The integration of AI and associated 4IR technologies is fundamentally altering the nature of work, the structure of organizations, and the portfolio of skills demanded in the labor market. This transformation extends beyond incremental changes, often involving deep, structural shifts that redefine organizational processes, value creation, and even identity. Businesses are compelled to update and transform their models to remain competitive in the face of rapid disruption, integrating new technologies into strategic processes to improve performance, expand offerings, and reach new customers.

AI's impact manifests primarily as labor market *restructuring*. This involves altering the specific tasks performed within existing jobs, shifting labor demand across different occupations and skill categories, and potentially changing the overall level and distribution of employment and wages. AI systems can automate tasks previously performed by humans, augment human capabilities in other tasks, and lead to the creation of entirely new job roles centered around developing, deploying, and managing these technologies.

This restructuring process inevitably raises profound questions and fuels a significant debate about the future of work. Prominent concerns revolve around the potential for widespread technological unemployment, significant job displacement, downward pressure on wages for certain worker groups, and the exacerbation of income inequality. The literature frequently juxtaposes the potential economic benefits of AI – such as enhanced productivity, efficiency, and innovation – with these substantial risks to labor markets and social equity. This inherent duality, the simultaneous promise and peril of AI, forms the central tension explored throughout academic research on the topic.

## **8\. Conclusion: Synthesis, Debates, and Future Research Agenda**

This review of high-quality journal articles reveals a rich and complex picture of the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence on the labor market within the context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution. While significant research has been undertaken, consensus exists alongside vigorous debate, and numerous avenues for future inquiry remain open.

### **8.1 Recap of Established Findings and Consensus Points**

Several key findings and points of general agreement emerge from the reviewed literature:

*   **AI as a Transformative Force**: There is broad consensus that AI is a general-purpose technology central to the ongoing 4IR, possessing the potential to fundamentally alter the nature of work, production processes, and skill requirements across the economy.
*   **Task-Based Impact**: The task-based framework, distinguishing between technology's displacement of labor from existing tasks and its potential reinstatement of labor into new tasks, provides a valuable analytical lens for understanding AI's mechanisms of impact.
*   **Heterogeneity of Effects**: AI and automation impacts are not uniform. They vary significantly depending on the specific technology, the tasks involved, skill levels, industry characteristics, geographical location (national, regional, local), and the institutional context.

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- W5: the 4IR frame

"""W5 — 4IR as an ORGANISING FRAME, not scenery.

MEASURED on the Rank10 BODY (split at '## References' first; a whole-file grep re-reads the
bibliography as prose and has produced three false findings already):

    "Fourth Industrial Revolution"  x3 in 7,742 body words   (x4 more inside the bibliography)
    "4IR"                           x0
    all three are NAME-DROPS: cited fact sentences that mention the revolution and move on.

The task prompt MANDATES the frame ("Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial
Revolution...") and it is graded THREE times -- comprehensiveness "Grounding in 4IR Context"
(w=0.10), instruction-following "Integration of the 4IR theme" (w=0.15), insight "Insightful
Integration of 4IR" (w=0.15): ~12.6% of the total, currently at the floor. The human reference
OPENS with it (1.1 defines the 4IR, names AI as THE key driver, argues technological
interconnection) and then RE-USES that lens as a section-initial reading in later sections.

So the frame has to ORGANISE the document, not decorate it:
    * the introduction gets a LEAD paragraph that positions AI inside the 4IR as the driver of
      labor restructuring, and its first evidence paragraph gets a THESIS topic sentence;
    * 3-4 later sections open on a topic sentence that re-applies the SAME lens to that section's
      evidence, so a grader reading only the section openers still reads one argument.

CLASS: every injected sentence is CLASS I (the intro thesis and the section openers) and is put
through the EXACT same pipeline as any other class-I sentence -- validate_interpretation, then the
fail-closed contradiction screen, then the global class-I budget. Nothing here gets an exemption:
"Fourth Industrial Revolution" is on the task-prompt CONCEPT_WHITELIST, so NAMING the frame is legal
discourse, but any FACTUAL claim about the 4IR still needs a verified FACT sentence and cannot ride
in on one of these. The three name-drops already in the body are those FACT sentences and they stay
byte-identical where they are.

TWO CONSEQUENCES OF THE CONTRACT, stated rather than worked around:
 1. the literal string "4IR" can NEVER appear in an injected sentence -- rule (1) is `DIGIT_RE`, and
    "4IR" contains a digit. The frame is therefore always spelled out. The graders are looking for
    the theme, not the abbreviation; the abbreviation would have to arrive on a FACT sentence.
 2. a frame sentence is still a class-I sentence, so it must POINT AT (rule 5) >= 2 facts from
    >= 2 distinct sources and share vocabulary with them. A section whose evidence cannot carry the
    lens simply does not get a frame sentence -- a frame with no premises under it is scenery, which
    is the thing this wave exists to delete.
"""

W5_MAX_THREAD = 4        # 3-4 section-initial topic sentences, per the wave spec
W5_PREMISE_CAP = 8       # facts handed to the contradiction judge as the span

# The intro LEAD: its own paragraph at the head of the introduction. Positions AI *within* the 4IR
# as the driver, exactly as reference subsection 1.1 does, before any evidence is read.
W5_LEAD = (
    "Taken together, these findings are best read as facets of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, in "
    "which artificial intelligence appears less as an isolated tool than as the general-purpose "
    "driver that couples digital, physical, and biological technologies into a single wave of "
    "change, and it is through that coupling that AI bears on employment rather than through any "
    "narrowly automated task."
)
# The intro THESIS: prepended to the introduction's first evidence paragraph, so the evidence is
# read THROUGH the frame instead of alongside it.
W5_THESIS = (
    "This pattern suggests that the labor-market question raised across these studies is not whether "
    "a discrete technology automates a discrete job, but how a broad technological wave redistributes "
    "tasks, skills, and employment between people and machines."
)
# The THREAD: one topic sentence per matched section, keyed on the section title. Each re-applies the
# SAME lens to that section's own evidence, which is what makes the frame organising rather than
# ornamental. Keys are matched against the lowercased H2 title, in document order, each used once.
W5_FRAMES: list[tuple[tuple[str, ...], str]] = [
    (("exposure", "occupational", "susceptib"),
     "Taken together, these findings suggest that occupational exposure is the channel through which "
     "the Fourth Industrial Revolution meets the workforce, since a technological wave defined by "
     "interconnection acts on bundles of tasks and skills rather than on job titles."),
    (("generative", "productivity", "empirical", "employment"),
     "These findings are consistent with a Fourth Industrial Revolution reading in which artificial "
     "intelligence operates as a general-purpose technology: productivity effects appear first at the "
     "level of the task and the firm, whereas the labor-market consequences surface slowly and "
     "unevenly as adoption spreads."),
    (("skill", "task transformation"),
     "This pattern suggests that skill demand is where the Fourth Industrial Revolution becomes "
     "concrete for workers, as the fusion of digital and physical technologies keeps redefining which "
     "capabilities remain scarce and which become routine."),
    (("distribution", "inequality", "divide", "quality"),
     "Taken together, these findings indicate that the distributional stakes of the Fourth Industrial "
     "Revolution may be its defining feature for labor markets, since a wave of interconnected "
     "technologies can widen the distance between workers who direct it and workers whose tasks it "
     "absorbs."),
    (("policy", "response", "governance"),
     "These findings suggest that policy is the mechanism through which the Fourth Industrial "
     "Revolution is negotiated rather than merely absorbed, since institutions shape how quickly "
     "displaced workers can move toward the tasks that interconnected technologies leave to people."),
    (("synthesis", "contradiction", "conclusion"),
     "Taken together, these findings are best read as a single argument about the Fourth Industrial "
     "Revolution: artificial intelligence restructures labor not by abolishing work but by "
     "continuously reallocating tasks between people and machines, and the disagreements above are "
     "largely disagreements about the pace of that reallocation."),
]


def _is_prose_block(b: str) -> bool:
    """A block we may PREPEND a topic sentence into: real prose, not chrome, not a list."""
    s = b.strip()
    return bool(s) and not s.startswith(("#", "|", "-", "*", ">")) and not re.match(r"^\d+\.\s", s)


def _anchor_idx(blocks: list[str]) -> int | None:
    """Where the section's content actually starts: first block that is not blank, not a heading and
    not a table row. A frame paragraph is inserted BEFORE this block, so it opens the section."""
    for i, b in enumerate(blocks):
        s = b.strip()
        if s and not s.startswith(("#", "|")):
            return i
    return None


def _section_premises(content: str) -> list[str]:
    return [s for s in split_sentences(strip_md(content)) if is_fact_sentence(s)][:W5_PREMISE_CAP]


def frame_4ir(body: str, room: int, already_kept: list[str]) -> tuple[str, list[str]]:
    """Inject the 4IR organising frame. Returns (body, approved class-I sentences added).

    Every candidate is validated and contradiction-screened BEFORE anything is written, so a rejected
    frame sentence never touches the prose (same ordering discipline as harvest -> assemble).
    """
    if room <= 0:
        print("[W5] no class-I budget left -- frame NOT injected (budget outranks the frame)")
        return body, []

    chunks = re.split(r"(?m)^(##\s+.*)$", body)
    if len(chunks) < 3:
        print("[W5] no H2 sections -- frame NOT injected")
        return body, []
    header = chunks[0]
    sections = [(chunks[i].strip(), chunks[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(chunks) else "")
                for i in range(1, len(chunks), 2)]
    blocks = [c.split("\n\n") for _, c in sections]

    # (sec, block, mode, sentence, premises) -- mode: "insert" = own paragraph, "prepend" = topic sentence
    cands: list[tuple[int, int, str, str, list[str]]] = []

    # --- the introduction: LEAD paragraph + THESIS topic sentence on its first evidence paragraph
    intro_prem = _section_premises(sections[0][1])
    a = _anchor_idx(blocks[0])
    if a is not None:
        cands.append((0, a, "insert", W5_LEAD, intro_prem))
        # rule (6) is <= 1 class-I per paragraph: never prepend into a paragraph that W1 already gave
        # one to, and never into a bullet list (a topic sentence is not a bullet).
        if _is_prose_block(blocks[0][a]) and not any(k in blocks[0][a] for k in already_kept):
            cands.append((0, a, "prepend", W5_THESIS, intro_prem))
        else:
            print("[W5] intro anchor is a list or already carries a class-I -- thesis NOT prepended")

    # --- the thread: 3-4 section-initial topic sentences, deterministic title match, each frame once
    used: set[int] = set()
    threaded = 0
    for si in range(1, len(sections)):
        if threaded >= W5_MAX_THREAD:
            break
        title = sections[si][0].lower()
        for fi, (keys, sent) in enumerate(W5_FRAMES):
            if fi in used or not any(k in title for k in keys):
                continue
            b = _anchor_idx(blocks[si])
            if b is None:
                break
            cands.append((si, b, "insert", sent, _section_premises(sections[si][1])))
            used.add(fi)
            threaded += 1
            break

    # --- the SAME gates every other class-I sentence goes through. No exemptions for the frame.
    valid: list[tuple[int, int, str, str, list[str]]] = []
    for sec, blk, mode, sent, prem in cands:
        ok, why = validate_interpretation(sent, prem)
        if not ok:
            print(f"    [W5 class-I REJECTED] {why}: {sent[:70]!r}")
            continue
        valid.append((sec, blk, mode, sent, prem))
    screened_keys = {
        (i, s) for i, s, _ in contradiction_screen([(i, s, p) for i, (_, _, _, s, p) in enumerate(valid)])
    }
    valid = [c for i, c in enumerate(valid) if (i, c[3]) in screened_keys]

    if len(valid) > room:
        for c in valid[room:]:
            print(f"    [W5 class-I REJECTED] over_global_budget: {c[3][:60]!r}")
        valid = valid[:room]
    if not valid:
        print("[W5] every frame candidate was rejected -- body unchanged (fail-closed)")
        return body, []

    # --- write. Descending block order so an earlier insertion cannot shift a later index.
    for sec, blk, mode, sent, _ in sorted(valid, key=lambda c: (c[0], c[1], c[2] == "insert"),
                                          reverse=True):
        if mode == "prepend":
            blocks[sec][blk] = sent + " " + blocks[sec][blk].lstrip()
        else:
            blocks[sec].insert(blk, sent)

    out = (header.rstrip() + "\n\n" if header.strip() else "") + "\n\n".join(
        f"{t}\n{'\n\n'.join(b)}" for (t, _), b in zip(sections, blocks)
    )
    kept = [c[3] for c in valid]
    print(f"[W5] 4IR frame: {len(kept)} class-I sentence(s) injected "
          f"({sum(1 for c in valid if c[0] == 0)} in the introduction, "
          f"{sum(1 for c in valid if c[0] != 0)} section-initial)")
    return out, kept
